last_item: store/schema single-row CHECK (id = 1) on the instance + auth singleton tables (internal/store/schema.go:15,33) — every accessor hardcodes WHERE id = 1 (instance.go:17,60; auth.go:17,65,86,105), so a second row would silently diverge (GetInstance/GetAuth keep reading id=1 while an id!=1 row lives on unseen); the DB-level CHECK is the only guard making a second row impossible and NOTHING tested it — the existing COUNT(*)=1 assertions all write through the id=1 accessors so they'd still pass with the CHECK dropped (band-1 correctness)
last_result: SHIPPED (test only, no bug): added TestSingleRowCheckRejectsSecondIdentityRow to internal/store/store_test.go, table-driven over instance + auth. Drives a RAW INSERT of a non-1 id via s.DB() and asserts the DB refuses it with 'CHECK constraint failed'. Positive control (id=1 accepted) proves the row is otherwise fully valid, so the id!=1 rejection can only be the CHECK, not a stray NOT NULL/type failure (false-green guard). Probes id=2 (id=1 row present -> proves CHECK not PK uniqueness), id=0, and id=-1. No bug: the CHECK is correct; the test locks the contract. Mutation-proven (perl apply + git checkout revert, schema.go restored clean each time): drop CHECK on instance, drop on auth, weaken = 1 -> >= 1, and weaken = 1 -> id * id = 1 each red the matching subtest. The id * id = 1 mutation is exactly why id=-1 is probed: it admits a negative second identity row while still rejecting 0 and 2, so an id=0/id=2-only test stayed green against it. Review: code-reviewer clean (verified column lists are otherwise-valid, error string isolates CHECK from UNIQUE/type). test-skeptic found the id*id=1 escape on the first draft (id=-1 blind spot); added the id=-1 assertion before commit and re-verified it catches the escape on both tables. Gates green (gofmt clean all files, go vet exit 0, go test ./internal/store exit 0 + full ./... green earlier, CGO_ENABLED=0 static build); web untouched; e2e not needed (pure unit).
notes: Never-ending hardening loop. Bias: (A) prove vibe-coded code actually does what it claims with tests that fail if behavior breaks; (P) preflight every risky/destructive/external op and fail loud+actionable BEFORE acting; (D) tighten defaults + self-heal so a bad config can't wedge Postgres. Re-seed the backlog via a parallel audit panel (Mode S) when it runs thin — this loop does NOT self-complete. Shared defaults live in scripts/ralph/DEFAULTS.md; real sm behavior at /primary01/git/server-management/src/sm/. Run go/make outside the sandbox if snap-confine blocks it.
north_star: An indie hacker runs a rock-solid Postgres entirely from the indiepg UI on best defaults — never loses data, never gets wedged, never confused. Every risky path is preflighted, every failure is loud and actionable, every claim is covered by a test that would fail if the behavior broke.
